



This lecture will cover prediction for (Simple)  Linear Regression.

## Prediction from Regression Models

Simple linear regression models can be used to predict the response at any given value of the predictor.

Nonetheless, we should beware predicting far beyond the range of the data

Point predictions should be accompanied by corresponding
    prediction intervals, providing a range of plausible
    values.

## The Basics of Prediction

Suppose that we want to predict the response when the predictor
    *x* takes the value *x~0~*.

  - 
We can denote the prediction by $\hat{y_0}$, called "y hat".

Our model for the corresponding response, $Y_0$, is


$$Y_0 = \mu_0 + \varepsilon_0$$ where
    $$\mu_0 =E[Y_0] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_0$$
is the expected value of $Y_0$.   It is natural to estimate $\mu_0$ by

$$\hat \mu_0 = \hat \beta_0 + \hat \beta_1 x_0$$

We have no information about the error $\varepsilon_0$ so estimate by its expected  value as 0. 

Thus a point prediction of $Y_0$ is 
$$\begin{aligned}
    \hat y_0 = \hat \mu_0 
     =\hat \beta_0 + \hat \beta_1 x_0\end{aligned}$$

This should be fairly intuitive --- we just read the prediction from the fitted regression line.

## Prediction Versus Estimation of Mean Response

Suppose however we expect there to be several observations at $x= x_0$.  We want to estimate the mean response $E[Y| x=x_0]=  \mu_0$ for all the predictions.

Again the  natural estimate is  $\hat \mu_0 = \hat \beta_0 + \hat \beta_1 x_0$. 

Hence  point prediction is the same as estimating the mean response.

However, differences arise if we want to construct corresponding confidence    intervals.

## Confidence Interval for the Mean Response


The standard error for $\hat \mu_0$ is

$$SE(\hat \mu_0) = s \sqrt{ \frac1n + \frac{(x_0 - \bar x)^2}
    {s_{xx}} } \; .$$
    
Both the terms under the square root go towards zero as we get larger and larger samples. i.e, $SE(\hat\mu_0)$  is small for large $n$.

It can be shown that the sampling distribution of $\hat \mu_0$ is
    defined by 

$$\frac{ \hat \mu_0 - \mu_0 }{SE(\hat \mu_0)} \sim
    t_{n-2}$$

Hence a $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_0$ is
    given by 

$$\left ( ~~\hat \mu_0 - t(\alpha/2)~ SE(\hat \mu_0), \; 
    \hat \mu_0 + t(\alpha/2) ~SE(\hat \mu_0) ~~\right )$$ 

where $t(\alpha/2)$ is a critical value from the *t* distribution with *df=n-2*.


This confidence interval provides a range of plausible values for  the mean response $\hat \mu_0$ and the interval gets narrower as we gather larger samples.    In practice we will not need to use these formulas:  R will produce the confidence intervals for us. 

## Prediction Intervals

However when it comes to finding the **prediction error** for *y~0~*, we must
    take into account the *additional variation* among individuals expressed by the $\varepsilon_0$ term:

$$\mbox{var}(\hat{y_0}) = \mbox{var}(\hat{\mu_0}) + \mbox{var}(\varepsilon_0) 
    = [SE(\hat{\mu_0})]^2 + \sigma^2$$

Replacing $\sigma^2$ by its estimate *s^2^*, we get the
    prediction error of $\hat y_0$ as

$$PE(\hat y_0) = s \sqrt{1+ ~ \frac1n + \frac{(x_0 - \bar{x})^2}{s_{xx} 
    }} \; .$$

The *1 +* means that the standard error for prediction never goes to zero. 
A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ prediction interval for *y~0~* is then

$$\left ( ~~\hat{y_0} - t(\alpha/2)~ PE(\hat{y_0})~, \; \hat{y_0} + 
    t(\alpha/2)~ PE(\hat y_0) ~~\right )$$

As the name suggests, the probability that *y~0~* will actually lie  in this prediction interval is $1-\alpha$.
Again, we will not need to calculate these intervals by hand: R will do it for us. 

## Boiling Point Data

We will use data on boiling point in degrees Fahrenheit (*y*) and pressure in inches of mercury (*x*), collected during an expedition in the Alps.
Data source: A Handbook of Small Data Sets by Hand, Daly, Lunn, McConway and Ostrowski.

![something is boiling in a beaker.](../graphics/boiling.jpg) []()

## Prediction for Boiling Point Data in R

`r xfun::embed_file("../../data/boiling.csv")`


```{r getBoilData, echo=-1, eval=-2}
Boil <- read.csv(file="../../data/boiling.csv", header=TRUE) 
Boil <- read.csv(file="https://r-resources.massey.ac.nz/161221/data/boiling.csv", header=TRUE) 
Boil.lm <- lm(BPt~Pressure, data=Boil)
summary(Boil.lm)
```

To get the predicted values we set $x_0$ (which can be more than one value) in an object called a  data.frame,  then invoke the *predict( )* function based on an existing linear model. 

```{r prediction}
x0 <- data.frame(Pressure=c(27,32))
predict(Boil.lm,newdata=x0,interval="prediction",level=0.95)
```

Similarly we can calculate a confidence interval for the mean value of *Y* at these Pressure values.  The intervals are much narrower, as expected. 

```{r confidence}
predict(Boil.lm,newdata=x0,interval="confidence",level=0.95)
```

## Fitted Line for Boiling Point Data


```{r BoilFittedLine}
ggplot(Boil, mapping = aes(y=BPt, x=Pressure)) +geom_point() + 
geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + 
labs(ylab="Boiling Point", xlab="Pressure")
```

Model fit looks good so we should expect fairly tight prediction intervals.





## Conclusions for Boiling Point Data

Fitted model is $$E[\mbox{BPt}] = `r round(coef(Boil.lm)[1], 1)`  + `r round(coef(Boil.lm)[2], 3)`~\mbox{Pressure}$$

Pressure explains over 99% of the variation in boiling point.

When pressure is 27 inches of mercury, predicted temperature is 206.6 degrees Fahrenheit with 95% prediction interval (205.7, 207.6).

When pressure is 32 inches of mercury, prediction is 216.2 with     95% prediction interval (215.0, 217.3).

## A fitted line plot with prediction interval

It is quite useful to plot the prediction interval on the scatterplot.   The following home-made function does this for a single *x* and *y* variable. It assumes *x* and *y* are in an object *xydata = data.frame(x,y)*.  Optionally, it fits a polynomial in *x*. 

```{r FittedLinePlot}
FittedLinePlot = function(xydata, polyn=1){ 
x= xydata[,1] ;  y= xydata[,2]
varname=names(xydata)
model = lm(y~ poly(x,polyn))
pred.x = data.frame( x =min(x) + (0:100)*(max(x)-min(x) )/100)
pred.y =predict(model, newdata = pred.x, interval = "prediction")
matplot(pred.x, pred.y ,  lty=c(1,2,2), col=c(1,2,2), type="l",xlab =varname[1],ylab=varname[2])
matpoints(x,y, pch=1) 
datapred = predict(model, newdata = data.frame(x), interval = "prediction")
cbind(xydata,datapred)
}

FittedLinePlot(data.frame(Boil$Pressure,Boil$BPt) )
```

This shows there is one point which would be regarded as deviating unusually from the line. However the interval is designed to include 95% of new data, so having just one point, just outside the prediction bounds, is not a problem. 

Graphing the prediction interval is also useful for highlighting heteroscedasticity (unequal spread around the line.)
What we want the points to more or less fill up the vertical space between the prediction bounds and only go outside them in a random fashion. 

The NZISSURF data on Income vs Hours violates this. What we see in fact is that the Income data are closely clustered around the fitted line on the left, well inside the prediction bounds. But on the right the Incomes spill out over the prediction bounds above and also below the line. There are more points exceeding the bounds above the line than below, and by a bigger distance, which suggests the residuals are right-skewed.

```{r NZISSURF FitLinePlot}
NZISSURF =read.csv(file="../../data/NZISSURF.csv",header=TRUE)

attach(NZISSURF)
NZISflp= FittedLinePlot( data.frame(Hours,Income) )
```

The FittedLinePlot shows another, more subtle, problem with the model. If the model 
$$y \sim N(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x, \sigma^2)$$
was correct, then the space between the bounds at the left should be able to be filled up with data.  But in fact the lower bound goes far into the negative values, and no incomes can be negative.  So the basic assumption of the Prediction Interval is wrong. 

So what can we do about this?  Answer: transform the data. 
That is our next topic. 

As a final illustration, consider some data on the yield of a planting of Onions vs the density (how closely together they are planted). Low density means individual plants can grow big:  high density means they cannot.  

We will try fitting a cubic polynomial. 

```{r Onions}
Onions= read.csv(file="../../data/Onions.csv",header=TRUE)
attach(Onions)
Onions.flp = FittedLinePlot(data.frame(density,yield), polyn=3)
```

The cubic polynomial interpolates the data ok, but is not a good model.  The fact that the fitted curve bends sharply downwards at the right means it would be a terrible model for extrapolation.  We will see a better model next time. 


## Test your understanding

Use the model formulae for prediction intervals above to prove to yourself that:

1. The prediction interval is wider than the confidence interval for a given *x~0~*.
3. the intervals get wider the farther *x~0~* gets from $\bar{x}$.
4. the intervals get wider when the level of confidence for the interval increases.

```{r extraWork}
x0 <- data.frame(Pressure=c(27:32))
predict(Boil.lm, newdata=x0, interval="prediction", level=0.95)
predict(Boil.lm, newdata=x0, interval="confidence", level=0.95)
predict(Boil.lm, newdata=x0, interval="confidence", level=0.99)
```
